name: Electric Universe
description: The Electric Universe (EU) is a fringe cosmology proposing that electricity plays a far more significant role in the universe than conventionally acknowledged by mainstream science. Proponents argue that gravity is a relatively weak force compared to electromagnetism and that many phenomena currently attributed to gravity, dark matter, or black holes are better explained by electrical forces, plasma behavior, and Birkeland currents (naturally occurring electric currents moving along magnetic field lines). The EU model suggests that stars are not powered by nuclear fusion but by external electrical currents flowing through space, and that planetary surfaces exhibit evidence of massive electrical discharge events in the past, shaping landscapes in ways misinterpreted by traditional geological models. These events are often linked to catastrophic events described in mythology. The EU theory originated in the late 20th century, drawing on the work of plasma physicist Hannes Alfvén and electrical engineer Ralph Juergens. Key evidence cited includes the filamentary structure observed in nebulae, the surface features of planets and moons (interpreted as electrical scarring), and the behavior of plasma in laboratory experiments. Mainstream science strongly rejects the Electric Universe theory. It is criticized for lacking rigorous mathematical frameworks, contradicting established physical laws, selectively interpreting data, and failing to provide testable predictions that can be verified through observation or experiment. The standard model of cosmology, supported by extensive observational evidence and theoretical consistency, relies on gravity and nuclear fusion as the primary forces shaping the universe. While acknowledging the role of electromagnetism in specific astrophysical phenomena (e.g., magnetic fields around stars), mainstream scientists argue that it is not the dominant force as claimed by EU proponents. The EU is considered pseudoscience by the scientific community. Its interpretations of geological and astronomical data are often deemed speculative and unsupported by empirical evidence.
subtitle: Electricity as the dominant cosmic force?

claims.claim: Stars are powered by external electrical currents, not nuclear fusion.
claims.evidence: Observations of surface phenomena on stars, such as coronal heating and X-ray emissions, that proponents claim cannot be adequately explained by standard fusion models.
claims.criticism: Standard stellar models based on nuclear fusion accurately predict observed stellar properties, including luminosity, temperature, and elemental abundances. Coronal heating is explained by magnetic reconnection, a well-established process.

claims.claim: Planetary surface features are primarily shaped by electrical discharge events, not gradual geological processes.
claims.evidence: Similarities between laboratory electrical discharge patterns and observed features like canyons, craters, and rilles on planets and moons.
claims.criticism: Traditional geological processes such as volcanism, erosion, and impact cratering adequately explain the vast majority of planetary surface features. The similarities to electrical discharge patterns are superficial and lack quantitative support.

claims.claim: Gravity is a relatively weak force and electromagnetism is the dominant force in the universe.
claims.evidence: The filamentary structure of nebulae and galaxies, which proponents claim is evidence of Birkeland currents.
claims.criticism: While electromagnetism plays a role in shaping plasma structures in space, gravity is the dominant force on large scales, dictating the overall structure and dynamics of galaxies and the universe. The filamentary structures can be explained by magnetohydrodynamic processes within the framework of standard cosmology.

keyFigures.name: Wal Thornhill
keyFigures.role: Leading proponent and popularizer of the Electric Universe theory

keyFigures.name: Donald Scott
keyFigures.role: Electrical engineer and proponent of the Electric Sky model

keyFigures.name: Hannes Alfvén
keyFigures.role: Plasma physicist whose work is cited by EU proponents, though he did not endorse the full EU model

keyFigures.name: Dave Talbott
keyFigures.role: Author and popularizer of the Electric Universe through the Thunderbolts Project

keyFigures.name: Phil Plait
keyFigures.role: Astronomer and science communicator who has extensively debunked Electric Universe claims

mythologyConnections: Claims that ancient myths describe electrical phenomena and catastrophic electrical events in the sky
Interpretations of petroglyphs as depictions of plasma discharges

timeline.event: Emergence of the Electric Universe theory, drawing on the work of Alfvén and Juergens
Popularization of the Electric Universe through websites, books, and conferences
Continued debate and criticism from the mainstream scientific community

sources.author: NASA
sources.work: Various publications on plasma physics and astrophysics
sources.title: NASA
